Hey, it's Andre and Sean. Today we are going to be talking about bridge emails. We're going to start off by having Andre just give us an overview and then I'll probably, I have a few things to add, but mostly I will play the role of the audience and ask questions and make sure that we're answering any questions that you might have as we go through this. So Andre, why don't you give us an overview of what we're looking at? Alrighty, so there's a few scenarios here. We'll start off on the top left and then just work our way through this. Actually, I'll start off in the centerpiece here because this image here represents an email. So this represents a hard bridge email. And if you've been in our world for any period of time, you would have seen us sending a lot of these. So hard bridge email, there's context that creates context for a click. So the click is active intent based on what's been shared of it. So none of this is blind. So hard bridge is this email that paints the picture for what's going to happen after the click. So yeah, and then we've got a few use cases yet that we can unpack in a sec. Do you have any questions from this piece yet? Yeah, I'll give people a real example just to dimensionalize this a little bit. So for example, the first time when we had enrollments for the art of email, when it was released in the fall of 2021, everyone who eventually saw the enrollment, saw the promotion, there are different promotions depending on if somebody already owned autoresponder madness and they were getting the upgrade to free, to the art of email for free, or if they didn't own autoresponder madness. And we were talking about the art of email, but each of those people who saw that promotion saw it on the other side of a bridge email. We sent two, in fact, the first one was to say, this thing is coming. And if you're interested, raise your hand. And the bridge email provides that context to say something different is about to happen and that different thing that's about to happen involves selling generally, but not always. And we want explicit permission to show you that. We want you to tell us that you're interested. And by clicking on something, they've then raised their hand and said, yep, that's, and that's where the name bridge email comes from. The bridge, it's the bridge between what we're generally doing with relationship building to this transition to doing something else, usually about a very specific topic. And it doesn't have to be selling. We can have a bridge email to a soap opera series that further explains, like we have at the bottom here, a showcase series. So we could raise their hand and say, hey, I'm interested in paid traffic in general. And they're not giving you permission to sell them something, but they are giving you permission. They're essentially saying, OK, this you've given me some context to understand that paid traffic in this example is useful to me. I'd like to learn more. And then the click and the bridge email is the indication that I'm interested in this topic. And that gives us permission to then take them on a journey about that topic. So I think I think that covers that a little bit. So why don't you move on to just putting into a larger context? Yeah. And so in the details of this lesson, we unpack a few reasons. But one of them that I want to draw attention to is the fact that when someone clicks that commitment that they're making, it focuses it focuses their attention because now they aware that something's about to happen, as opposed to it just being passive. And then like emails just arrive and by focusing people's attention on about on something that's about to happen, especially when it's a promotion. It's it's it's a very different dynamic. So we're going to talk about let's start with let's let's move to this this image is hypothetical scenario on the left hand side because there's a few versions of this as this presented over here. Most of us. So and also let me just explain what SB means because you won't see this in the main lesson. SB represents what we call a soft bridge or what we think about this version of a of a bridge email is a soft bridge and this is a hard bridge. Now hard bridges are the most common everywhere because this really embodies this idea that we are asking people to raise their hand and we've been respectful about their attention. So in almost every case, it'll always be a hard bridge. But in some cases, there's this thing called a soft bridge, which so I'll unpack what that means now. So in this scenario, RBS over here would be a product focused entry point. So using an example that we are familiar with is let's say somebody's on the email product page over here and there's a button to bar. So you know, somebody can bar, but they choose not to buy and they choose to add themselves to the email list to to to to learn more about the art of email through a, you know, through an email delivered series. So that would represent their intent. That's that's them raising their hand because they want to hear about this thing. It's not a general obvious. It's a very specific one, which is why over here, a soft bridge, we're not asking people to raise their hand again before we transition, transition to a promotion. So in that context, them opting in is them raising their hand. There's the relationship boarding series that is more product focused in that context. And then the SB over here would be a transition between the what's just happened and and a promotion. This is a story part of promotion in this context. In in our. So if you go to our website and you go to slash, subscribe our RBS, because that's a more general one. It's not a product focused one. We we share our philosophy inside of there. In that use case, then this would be an HB. This would be a hard bridge because we wouldn't want to just get to the end of something that's that's more general and suddenly move straight into a promotion. The we would have a hard bridge over here, which would be more an email that had a for us because we've got multiple courses would be a choose your own adventure. If it's for somebody that has one one product to say, hey, if you know, if you're interested in in, you know, taking this to the next level, click this link to raise your hand and then it would move into the promotion. So I won't move on just yet to this next piece. What are you thinking? What you're thinking about that? Yeah, so I'll put this into some context because I asked this question in the past too. So it's familiar to me. The critical distinction between a soft bridge and a hard bridge is explicit intent. So if like in our world, if somebody were to sign up for our emergent marketing newsletter, that that's a that's general interest. No one has said, hey, there's a specific course I'm interested in yet. So that relationship building series transitions to a value newsletter. And then we use hard bridge emails for someone to raise his or her hand because they didn't give us explicit permission about a product or a course in advance. So the hard bridge requires them to click on something and say, yes, I'm interested in this thing that you've just given me the context for the soft bridge is simply a transition email that suggests something is coming. So if using the example that Andre just gave, if someone comes over to come to our site and say, wow, that's out of email sounds really interesting or sphere of influence or the traffic engine or the durable business that sounds really interesting, I'd like to learn more about this thing. And they opt in at that point. Well, they've already told us they're interested in in the course. But that doesn't mean that we jump right to selling. We still believe it's relationships before transactions. So we have a relationship building series that is within the context of them saying, I want a relationship with you around this course. So we know they're we're not going to say, you know, hey, before we can tell you about the art of email, we need to take 10 steps back and tell you who we are. What we would do instead is we would say, okay, you in the context of you being interested in the art of email, for example, we're going to spend, you know, three, four, five, seven, 10 days or 10 emails building that relationship with you. And then there's going to be an email that transitions. That's why it's the soft bridge. It transitions between building that relationship, that being the main focus to now talking about the thing that you asked us about when you raised your hand and said you were interested. So it seems like it's a very subtle difference. But if what we're trying to do is to be as responsive and respectful as possible to our audience, that really goes two ways. One, if someone comes in with general interest, we need to ask them that they want to learn more about a specific product. But the inverse of that is true too. If someone comes in and says, hey, I'm interested in this course or this product, we don't make make them jump through hoops later to tell us again how interested they are. We're respecting the fact that they they raise their hand. And gave us their attention early. So that's really the distinction between the soft bridge and the hard bridge. All right, do you want to put this in further context? Yeah, OK, so let's. So at this point, somebody is now part of the value newsletter loop. So they're going to continue to receive value newsletters over time. It could live where the PLS is or if this is a proper time at time bound thing. That's what that would be. The hard bridge would move people into a into a full promotion in this one. It could move people into a into an evergreen promotion if you wanted to automate this whole this whole thread. And again, over here, there's another example in this in this email over here. There's a little hard bridge that goes to promotion. Yeah. So anything else to say about that? Yeah. And I think just I'll take sort of I'll zoom out for a second and reorient. So why do we do this at all? Like the same I'm imagining some people are watching this video or they're reading this thing. I'm just going to skip this. This is this is a necessary and it's this is so important and it's important because a bridge email does two things exceptionally well. It rewards attention. That's that's one of one of the two things that's critically important. When someone says, well, I'm interested in this, whatever it is that you've shown me in some context and they click on it, you're rewarding their attention because they're getting something that someone who doesn't raise his or her hand gets. And the value in maybe a soap opera series or a story powered promotion or a PLS or the eventual course that they they see and they can buy all of that rewards that the click that rewards their raising their hand and saying right now in the in the context that I find myself in, this seems like something that could be interesting to me and I want to know more. And then the second thing that it does that's that's probably as obvious and equally important is it protects in attention. Right. And if you think about your audience has a finite amount of attention, there's only so many things we can pay attention to. And when some if we if everybody sees everything all the time, then we're not protecting the inattention of that. And in the theory as well, people will just not pay attention to it, but they still do. It's still it takes a little bit. It's still in their awareness. So the hard bridge in particular is a way to reward attention and protect inattention. And both of those things are incredibly important. When someone clicks and raises their hand, this is I think this is something that's right for me now. They get the experience of everything that follows. And for the person who doesn't, we are firewalling their attention for later when something does catch their attention and it is relevant to them. We haven't we haven't consumed any of their attention in the process. And that once once you wrap your head around how powerful that is, the scenarios with which to use the hard bridge and the soft bridge just I think they begin to emerge very quickly as you realize, well, I have I have the ability to give my audience a true behaviorally based, unique, choose your own adventure experience in my email world and the hard bridge makes that possible. It's the critical ingredient for that. And this is this is why the majority of of of promotions certainly PLS promotions and well, actually both of them most people never see them in the sense that we will send out a hard bridge to this email here, we would send this out to everyone and only those people that are interested will click and the rest of the world wouldn't even see it. Wouldn't see past this in the same way that is if somebody is still coming through an obvious they haven't been tagged as as available at that point. So if we send a hard bridge to everybody, well, it's everybody minus these people here because we haven't established our relationship with them and this needs to play out first. So only at this point here where this little tag is added over here, is this person now considered open to receive all of our newsletters, all of our hard bridges and any communication. And obviously at this point, they run through the own to your own adventure. But so. Yeah, so people don't always see promotions because of the way that this is this is rigged up and this and that's very intentional. So the final the final thing really is again, this is something that we impact in the story part promotion module, but in this context over here. This is footer links. So footer links can be quite descriptive. Descriptive. So let's, for example, say this footer link over here said out of email. Well, when somebody clicked that, which is they, you know, there is an element of intent there because they can see what it is. This hard bridge, there's still an email. It doesn't just go straight to promotion now because they've clicked the link that says out of email and suddenly they're going to receive emails from us, which is why it's important to have a hard bridge over here, which is which is an email that they'll receive after they've clicked this link saying, hey, you know, I noticed that you've clicked this link if you're interested in hearing more about the product, raise your hand. And we unpacked this example in more detail in the story part promotion lesson. So I won't reiterate it here, but other than the fact that these are the hard bridges. This is where you can use them. You can use them all over the place and your use cases will be different to ours and your context will be different to ours. So I think that's basically it. Yeah, I'll add two quick things to wrap up to here. One I've seen happen recently that I want to draw attention to. But the other is more of a higher level point that the bridge email is, you know, I've said before, I think that the soap opera series is the Swiss army knife of the art of email, the bridge email is two in many ways. And if you had, for example, if you have an audience and you have an idea for say a new course that you want to do and you're not really sure if people are interested in it or not, you don't have to create the course or have a PLS or do all of those things first. You could have in your value newsletter, a PS or something else. Hey, I'm thinking about doing a course on XYZ and I'm not exactly sure yet what to include. If this is interesting to you, let me know. And you can have a hard bridge link. You can have a link that says, you know, if this isn't it's interesting to you. Raise your hand here. And that could kick off, you know, a series of broadcasts, for example, where you're like, hey, this is, you know, Sean and Andre, we're thinking about doing this course on XYZ and we're just really looking for feedback at this point. So over the next couple of days, we're going to tell you what we're thinking in broad strokes over the three topics that we think we want to cover. But each day we would love it if you could just give us your feedback on that. Like that you're not selling anything there, right? You're getting a sense of what the audience is interested in. But it says, and that's just one of, you know, an infinite number of examples of how you can use this to say who in my audience is right now interested in what I'm thinking about or what I have to offer or what I may have to offer. I mean, it's such a valuable tool. And the last thing I'll mention, I noticed this a few times lately and I want to make sure that we're clear about our views on it. There seem to be really three camps of email marketing. You know, camp number one is everybody gets everything all the time, right? That's the majority of email marketers. Then there's this, what I would consider kind of a halfway camp, which is you give people the ability to opt out of what you're offering. So I'm on a particular email list where I see this a lot where it's like that person sends a lot of email and then once and a lot of those emails are related to some larger promotions. And then often those promotions will have somewhere in them and say, hey, if you're not interested in this, click this link to mute this promotion. Right? That's not what we're suggesting. That's kind of that requires, and I very rarely do that. Like I'm not like you're asking me to do something to turn down the noise, right? And then we're the third camp in this spectrum. We're saying we're not just going to assume everybody wants to hear from us and tell them they have to turn it off. We're going to instead require that they raise their hand to turn it on. And that may seem like it's more extreme, but it's important. It means that everybody who sees everything that follows has actively said, this is interesting to me right now, and that makes all of the difference. I think we covered a lot of ground here, Andre. Anything you want to say to wrap this up before we call it a day on this one? No, I think that basically sums it up. I will add one extra thing to what you've just said is so we ask people to raise their hand to receive something, especially any promotion, and then within that promotion, even though someone's raised their hand, because let's be honest, it's relatively easy for people to click links. We're all conditioned. So there are some cases where somebody will click and it'll segment them into a promotion. So we do the extra thing as well. And so then at the top of every single promotion is a way for somebody to remove themselves from that promotion if they've either segmented themselves by mistake, or they've just decided along the way, like if it's a 10 day promotion and they fabbed theirs into it and they just, you know, then they're done, they can just leave it, you know, at any point and we don't harass them. So doing both is is a nice way to respect someone's attention. That's the last thing I'll say. Sounds good. As always, thanks everybody and get back to work.
